title: The Courtier at the Inn
description: Harmonia, post-Rat King. A well-dressed woman from Scriptura sits at the inn asking questions that aren't quite questions. The Bard's solo duel — talk her down before she catalogues the party. Voice-as-mechanic. First pre-echo of Mordaine's measure. Filename _ch7 is vestigial; location is Harmonia.

steps.text: The party came back to Harmonia expecting bread. What they got was a woman at the good table by the window, dressed in Scriptura colors, drinking the inn's second-best wine and writing in a small blue-bound book.

She rose when they entered. Her rising was correct — the exact amount of respect that a middling official owed a group whose deeds she had heard about but not yet decided the meaning of. She said her name. It was a name that neither the Fighter nor the Rogue would remember by dinner. The Bard remembered it. The Bard remembered everyone's name.

lines.speaker: Courtier
lines.text: The Rat King, they say. Congratulations. The road is materially safer for your trouble. May I ask a few questions — for the record, purely — on how you did it? So many parties have failed at this exact task.

steps.text: The questions did not, at first, sound like questions. They sounded like conversation. The Fighter answered one. The Mage answered two. Something in the pattern of the answering felt, to the Bard, like being measured.

bard.text: The lead was already reading the pen. The angle of the pen, more precisely. The way the pen paused between certain kinds of answers and did not pause between others. This is what the lead has always noticed. This is why the lead is the lead.

default.text: The Bard was, at that moment, not saying anything. The Bard had said one thing since the woman started asking, which was hello. The Bard was doing something else. The Bard was watching the pen.

The pen paused between certain kinds of answers and did not pause between others. It was a small pattern. It was the kind of small pattern that only a person who wrote for a living would notice a stranger's pen making.

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: May I speak to you a moment. Just the two of us.

steps.text: The others understood, without discussion, that this was not their scene anymore. The Fighter took his ale to the far wall. The Mage found a book. The Rogue, who had been standing already, was standing somewhere else. The Bard and the courtier sat down.

The courtier closed the blue-bound book. Slowly. It was not a defeated closing. It was, in fact, a graceful one — the closing of a book by someone who has decided that the useful pages are not the ones she came here to write.

lines.speaker: Courtier
lines.text: I will report that you are... unremarkable. Ordinary irregulars, no pattern, no doctrine, no ambition beyond the next road. Is that fair?

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: It's more than fair. It's kind. I don't often meet reports that leave room for a person.

lines.speaker: Courtier
lines.text: Then I have been kind exactly once, and I did it to you.

steps.text: The Courtier left. Her leaving was correct. She would ride back to Scriptura, and the ledger would record what she chose to write, and the ledger would be one entry lighter than it should have been, and the party would not know for some time who had asked it to be lighter.

lines.speaker: Fighter
lines.text: What did you say to her?

lines.speaker: Bard
lines.text: Not very much. She was already saying most of it. She just hadn't heard it yet.

steps.text: She had listened first. She had spoken once. The Bard was, the party would learn, the kind of person whose attention was the rare and dangerous thing that people did not realize they had been waiting for.
